title: The Curse of Monkey Island
notes: The transition to cel-shaded animation was controversial but yielded one of the most **beautiful** adventure games ever made. Murray the Demonic Talking Skull is a legend.

title: The Wolf Among Us
notes: A gritty, neon-soaked fairy tale murder mystery. Bigby Wolf's chain-smoking detective routine is **legendary**. The synthwave soundtrack and comic book aesthetic perfectly capture Fabletown's corruption.

title: The Darkside Detective
notes: A bite-sized, incredibly funny take on the X-Files wrapped in chunky pixel art. Detective McQueen and Officer Dooley's banter is **legendary**, tackling gremlins and ghosts with pure incompetence.

title: The Excavation of Hob's Barrow
notes: A **masterclass** in folk horror. The isolated English moors drip with atmosphere and creeping dread. The slow burn narrative builds to one of the most memorable and haunting conclusions in modern adventure gaming.

title: Simon the Sorcerer
notes: A British comedy **classic** heavily inspired by Terry Pratchett and LucasArts. Simon is a sarcastic teenager thrown into a fantasy world, and his relentless cynical quips carry the entire adventure.

title: Simon the Sorcerer 2: The Lion, the Wizard and the Wardrobe
notes: Simon returns, even more cynical and fourth-wall-breaking than before. Sordid is back, and the puzzles remain delightfully absurd in this nostalgic slice of 90s Euro-adventure history.

title: Lamplight City
notes: A steampunk detective adventure where you can completely botch investigations and wrongly accuse people, but the story just keeps going. The lack of an inventory system is a bold, refreshing twist for the genre.

title: Whispers of a Machine
notes: A brilliant sci-fi Nordic noir from the creators of Kathy Rain. Vera's cybernetic augmentations offer multiple ways to solve puzzles, meaning your choices genuinely shape the investigation.

title: Kathy Rain
notes: A brilliant homage to 90s point-and-click adventures. Kathy is a sharp-tongued journalism major wrapped up in a Twin Peaks-esque supernatural mystery in her hometown. The pixel art is gorgeous and the voice acting is surprisingly stellar.

title: Zero Parades: For Dead Spies
notes: ZA/UM's mysterious follow-up project. If it has even a fraction of Disco Elysium's brain-melting prose, we're in for a wild ride.

title: The Secret of Monkey Island
notes: I am rubber, you are glue. The foundational text of insult sword fighting, establishing a comedic tone that defined an entire era of adventure games.

title: Beneath a Steel Sky
notes: Dave Gibbons' art direction creates a suffocating yet beautiful dystopian metropolis. Foster and Joey's banter is the beating heart of Union City.

title: Full Throttle
notes: "Whenever I smell asphalt, I think of Maureen. That's the last sensation I had before I blacked out; that thick smell of asphalt."

title: Day of the Tentacle
notes: The microwave hamster puzzle was weird.

title: What Remains of Edith Finch
notes: The cannery sequence...

title: Escape from Monkey Island
notes: The jump to 3D and tank controls was jarring for many. Monkey Kombat is arguably the most frustrating puzzle in the entire series, but the humor is still there.

title: Tales of Monkey Island
notes: Telltale's episodic approach breathed new life into the franchise after a long hiatus. The narrative arc across the five chapters is surprisingly emotional.

title: Zak McKracken and the Alien Mindbenders
notes: A weird and wonderful sci-fi comedy with multiple protagonists. The humor holds up, even if the dead-ends are punishing.

title: Sam & Max Hit the Road
notes: One of the funniest adventure games ever made. The freelance police's road trip across America is packed with bizarre roadside attractions and incredible voice acting.

title: Broken Sword: The Shadow of the Templars
notes: The game that taught a generation that a Parisian café vacation will inevitably end in explosions, clowns, and Templar conspiracies.

title: Indiana Jones and the Last Crusade
notes: Nazi-punching point-and-click goodness. The branching puzzle solutions were way ahead of their time, but navigating that zeppelin maze is still a nightmare.

title: Sanitarium
notes: A spectacularly twisted psychological horror trip. Waking up amnesiac with your face bandaged is just the start of the madness.

title: I Have No Mouth, and I Must Scream
notes: AM is one of the most terrifying AI villains ever conceived. A brutally dark, uncompromising descent into a post-apocalyptic digital hell.

title: STASIS: BONE TOTEM
notes: An underwater rig of pure body horror and tension. Switching between the characters creates some brilliant, claustrophobic puzzle-solving.

title: STASIS
notes: Isometric sci-fi horror that proves you don't need first-person jump scares to make your skin crawl. Gloriously gruesome.

title: Technobabylon
notes: Wadjet Eye delivering prime cyberpunk. Wetware, AI overlords, and genetic engineering wrapped in a brilliant neo-noir narrative.

title: Old Skies
notes: Time-traveling tourism goes horribly wrong. Wadjet Eye's foray into high-res art looks gorgeous, and the premise is pure sci-fi gold.

title: Loom
notes: No inventory, just musical spells. A beautifully weird, hauntingly short masterpiece that dared to be completely different from other LucasArts games.

title: The Silent Age
notes: Time-traveling via a pocket device to solve puzzles between a vibrant 70s and a desolate future. A sleek, minimalist gem.

title: Broken Age
notes: The Kickstarter darling that revived the genre. Part 1 was pure magic, even if Part 2 leaned a bit too hard into frustrating backtracking.

title: Unavowed
notes: Urban fantasy meets BioWare-style party mechanics in a point-and-click adventure. The best thing Wadjet Eye has ever produced.

title: Indiana Jones and the Fate of Atlantis
notes: The greatest Indiana Jones movie never made. Three different paths to play through? LucasArts was just flexing at this point.

title: Syberia
notes: A melancholic, atmospheric journey filled with clockwork automatons and Soviet-era decay. Kate Walker's transformation is incredibly compelling.

title: The Case of the Golden Idol
notes: The true successor to Obra Dinn. Filling out those mad libs of murder using pure deductive reasoning makes you feel like an absolute genius.

title: Monkey Island 2: LeChuck's Revenge
notes: The monkey wrench puzzle alone makes this infamous, but the ending remains one of the boldest, most debated conclusions in gaming history.

title: The Longest Journey
notes: An epic, sprawling narrative bridging magic and science. April Ryan is a legendary protagonist, and Arcadia feels like a living, breathing world.

title: Beautiful Desolation
notes: Isometric post-apocalyptic Africa with some of the most striking, bizarre environmental art you'll ever see.

title: Gabriel Knight: Sins of the Fathers
notes: Southern Gothic mystery dripping with atmosphere. Tim Curry's legendary, wildly inconsistent New Orleans accent is the cherry on top.

title: Gemini Rue
notes: A masterclass in rain-slicked, pixel-art cyberpunk noir. The twist regarding the two protagonists still hits hard.

title: Return to Monkey Island
notes: Ron Gilbert returning to his baby. The art style grew on everyone, and the ending is a beautiful, meta reflection on why we play these games.

title: Esoteric Ebb
notes: Disco Elysium meets D&D politics. Playing a cynical cleric navigating a fantasy election is exactly the kind of chaotic roleplay we need.

title: Mixtape
notes: Pure teenage nostalgia distilled into a playable mixtape. I can already hear the licensed indie soundtrack echoing in my head.

title: Life is Strange
notes: Hella emotional. Come for the time-traveling hipster photography, stay for the devastating trolley problems and the indie-folk soundtrack.

title: Firewatch
notes: The ultimate 'walkie-talkie simulator'. A beautiful, lonely hike through the Wyoming wilderness that hits you with a surprisingly grounded emotional gut-punch.

title: Machinarium
notes: A wordless masterpiece of rusty robot charm and deviously difficult puzzles. Amanita Design at its absolute peak.

title: Blade Runner
notes: Voxel-based replicant hunting! It captured the gritty, neon-drenched mood of the film perfectly, even if the real-time elements induced pure panic.

title: Heavy Rain
notes: PRESS X TO JASON! A messy, ambitious interactive drama that walked so Detroit: Become Human could run. The origami killer mystery still grips.

title: Oxenfree
notes: The walking-and-talking system is unparalleled. Nothing like a casual teen beach party turning into a terrifying, time-looping radio frequency nightmare.

title: Gone Home
notes: The game that sparked a thousand 'what is a game' debates. A masterful exercise in environmental storytelling and 90s riot grrrl nostalgia.

title: Detroit: Become Human
notes: David Cage's slickest, most branching flowchart of a game. Connor and Hank's buddy-cop dynamic carries the whole android revolution on its shoulders.

title: Beyond: Two Souls
notes: Elliot Page and Willem Dafoe trapped in a wild, chronologically fractured fever dream of CIA espionage and poltergeist action.

title: Norco
notes: A surreal, Southern Gothic cyberpunk trip through a decaying Louisiana. The writing is so thick and atmospheric you can practically smell the swamp.

title: The Dig
notes: Spielberg's sci-fi epic translated into a punishingly difficult adventure game. The atmosphere is top-notch, but that turtle bone puzzle is pure evil.

title: Thimbleweed Park
notes: A delightful, meta-heavy nostalgia trip to the golden age of SCUMM. The humor is sharp, the pixels are chunky, and the ending is beautifully divisive.

title: Disco Elysium
notes: I am a superstar cop. A staggering achievement in writing, where your own fragmented psyche is both your greatest asset and worst enemy.

title: Kentucky Route Zero
notes: More of a play than a game. Beautiful.

title: Grim Fandango
notes: Manny Calavera is iconic. A brilliant fusion of Aztec mythology and film noir. The tank controls are painful, but the writing is unparalleled.
